name: Pi d'en Xandri, La Torre Negra, Ermita Sant Medir, Turó de Valldaura – Collserola – Circular Route
name_original: Pi d'en Xandri, La Torre Negra, Ermita Sant Medir, Turó de Valldaura – Collserola – Ruta Circular
region_intro: Hiking around Cerdanyola Del Vallès offers direct access to the Serra de Collserola Natural Park, an expansive natural area characterized by Mediterranean forests, diverse terrain, and significant elevation changes. The region features wooded trails, wide paths, and some rocky sections. Numerous viewpoints provide vistas of the surrounding landscape, including the Vallès plain and the Collserola mountain range. Best hiking trails around Cerdanyola Del Vallès The most popular hiking route is Pi d'en Xandri, La Torre Negra, Ermita Sant Medir, Turó de Valldaura – Collserola – Circular Route, a 10.0 miles (16.2 km) trail that takes 4 hours 43 minutes to complete. This moderate route passes by a notable natural monument and a small mountain church. Another top favourite among local hikers is Collserola Viewpoints Loop: Forat del Vent and Portell de Valldaura, a moderate 7.9 miles (12.7 km) path. This trail offers panoramic views across the Collserola Natural Park. Local hikers also love the Cerdanyola del Vallès, Parc de la Riera, Bosc de Canaletes, Turo de Montcada, Mirador Valles Occidental – Circular Route, a 7.8 miles (12.6 km) trail leading through Bosc de Canaletes and past the Turo de Montcada hill, often completed in about 3 hours 36 minutes. Hiking around Cerdanyola Del Vallès is defined by the Mediterranean forests of Serra de Collserola Natural Park, diverse terrains including wooded paths and rocky sections, and numerous viewpoints. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy paths to more demanding routes. The routes in Cerdanyola Del Vallès are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 280 reviews. More than 1900 hikers have used komoot to explore Cerdanyola Del Vallès's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are there in Cerdanyola Del Vallès?
A: Cerdanyola Del Vallès offers a wide variety of hiking opportunities, with over 115 routes available to explore. These trails traverse the diverse landscapes of the Serra de Collserola Natural Park, providing options for all skill levels.

Q: What is the best time of year for hiking in Cerdanyola Del Vallès?
A: The Serra de Collserola Natural Park, accessible from Cerdanyola Del Vallès, is enjoyable year-round. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, while cooler winter days can also be ideal for hiking. Summer mornings or late afternoons are best to avoid the midday heat.

Q: Are there easy hikes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Cerdanyola Del Vallès provides numerous easy and accessible paths within the Serra de Collserola Natural Park, with 35 routes classified as easy. These trails are suitable for beginners and families looking for a gentle outdoor experience. Many routes are well-maintained and offer a chance to enjoy the Mediterranean forest.

Q: Are there more challenging hikes for experienced hikers?
A: For those seeking a greater challenge, Cerdanyola Del Vallès offers 5 difficult routes with significant elevation changes and potentially rocky sections within the Collserola Natural Park. These trails require good fitness and sure-footedness, providing a rewarding experience for experienced hikers.

Q: Are there circular hiking routes in the area?
A: Yes, many of the hiking routes around Cerdanyola Del Vallès are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point while exploring diverse scenery. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e950227660/pi-d-en-xandri-la-torre-negra-ermita-sant-medir-turo-de-valldaura-collserola-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pi d'en Xandri, La Torre Negra, Ermita Sant Medir, Turó de Valldaura – Collserola – Circular Route&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 16.2 km loop.

Q: What natural landmarks can I see while hiking?
A: While hiking, you can encounter several natural landmarks. The notable &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2630086" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pi d'en Xandri&lt;/a&gt;, an ancient pine tree, is a significant natural monument. You can also find natural springs like Font de Can Lloses and the tranquil Pantà de Vallvidrera reservoir within the park.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sites along the trails?
A: Yes, the trails often lead past historical and cultural sites. You can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e950227660/pi-d-en-xandri-la-torre-negra-ermita-sant-medir-turo-de-valldaura-collserola-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ermita Sant Medir&lt;/a&gt;, a small mountain church, or explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5324671" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canaletes Aqueduct&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features sites like the Iberian Museum and Village of Ca n'Oliver, offering a glimpse into ancient history.

Q: Are there any viewpoints with panoramic vistas?
A: Absolutely. The Serra de Collserola Natural Park is known for its numerous 'miradors' (viewpoints). You can enjoy stunning vistas of the Vallès plain, the Collserola mountain range, and even Barcelona and the Mediterranean Sea from various points. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e950497773/collserola-viewpoints-loop-forat-del-vent-and-portell-de-valldaura" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collserola Viewpoints Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically highlights spots like Portell de Valldaura.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Cerdanyola Del Vallès?
A: The hiking trails in Cerdanyola Del Vallès are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 4.5 stars from over 280 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse terrain, the beauty of the Mediterranean forests, and the accessibility to stunning viewpoints within the Serra de Collserola Natural Park.

Q: Can I hike with my dog in Cerdanyola Del Vallès?
A: Many trails within the Serra de Collserola Natural Park are dog-friendly, allowing you to enjoy the outdoors with your canine companion. It's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife, and to check specific trail regulations before heading out.

Q: Is public transport available to reach the trailheads?
A: Cerdanyola del Vallès is well-connected, and its proximity to the Serra de Collserola Natural Park means that many trailheads are accessible via public transport from the town. Local bus services often connect to various points around the park, making it convenient to start your hike without a car.

Q: Where can I park when hiking in Cerdanyola Del Vallès?
A: Parking is generally available at various entry points to the Serra de Collserola Natural Park from Cerdanyola Del Vallès. Look for designated parking areas near popular trailheads or park entrances. Some routes, like those starting from Parc de la Riera, may have nearby street parking or small lots.
tips:
The Mirador del Portell de Valldaura is one of several viewpoints over the Serra de Collserola Natural Park. It is very wide and through it you can access the S-2 Portell de Valldaura MTB trail:

Portell de Valldaura on Trailforks
https://www.trailforks.com/trails/portell-de-valldaura/

The story goes like this: On November 11, 1974, the Virgin Mary appeared to the seer Josefa Pugés. It was on an old fallen tree, saying these words 'I am the Virgin of Catalonia'. There have also been alleged healings of the Virgin.

The Pi d'en Xandri is one of the oldest pines in Catalonia and an ecological, cultural and historical symbol of Sant Cugat. It is estimated that he was born around the year 1774, at the time of Mozart, Kant and Napoleon Bonaparte, now about 248 years ago.

   It is a stone pine (pinus pinea) and is located in the Torre Negra rural park. It has a considerable height -23 meters- and a trunk of 3.20 meters in diameter. Its crown is 21 meters wide and has a peculiar shape, like a parasol, with a wayward, half-naked branch. The Pi d'en Xandri lost part of its branches due to the snow that accumulated on Christmas night in the year 62, when a formidable snowfall covered Catalonia in white. In 1997 some vandals tried to saw it off and set it on fire. It was necessary to graft a piece of live trunk so that the sap could circulate again and it was held with the trunks that surround it today to facilitate its healing.

The Black Tower is a Romanesque military construction in the municipality of San Cugat del Vallés, very well preserved, with a quadrangular plan and three stories high with a square tower attached to one corner of the building.

Since 1842 until today it is in private hands and belongs to the Rabadà family.

Some windows on the façade are geminated and the entrance door is formed by a semicircular arch.

Located in a natural setting between two streams, at the entrance to the Gausac valley on the border between the Collserola mountain range and the southern fields of the Vallés plain, with views over the entire horizon to the north.

Its origin dates back to the 12th century, in a castle named Ricanli or Ricard, built in the time of Abbot Ermengol and it seems that it was rebuilt between the 14th and 15th centuries. The Black Tower owes its current name to its dark-colored stone walls.

Its first known inhabitants were the Vilanova in the 12th century. Later it passed to the Palou family.

Throughout the Middle Ages it was the subject of disputes between the owners and the Sant Cugat monastery, which opposed its construction.

THE FARMHOUSE OF CAN BORRELL

The first historical references of human settlements in the place where Can Borrell is located today, take us back many centuries. Hau appointments that speak of finds of fragments of Iberian and Roman pottery in the surroundings.

Due to its location on the old road from Sant Cugat to Barcelona and a place of passage to Sant Medir, the farmhouse became an obligatory stopping place, first for the peasants of the region who brought their products to the capital, and later for hikers. and pilgrims from Sant Medir. On the day of this celebration, the house opened its front and back doors and became one more part of the road. On the way back, there was a dance and the last sardana del Aplec was danced. Until recently, the last dance of the festival was still held here.

The passage of hikers and other people meant that they began to serve lunch and drinks and, in view of its success, it became the restaurant that it is today.

This construction is located between the Pi d'en Xandri and the beautiful hermitage of Sant Medir, and just a few meters from the Forn Ibèric. Due to its situation and conservation, it is worth a visit. It is an easy and accessible route surrounded by natural charm. You can also appreciate the fauna of the area.

The Iberian oven of Sant Cugat del Vallès is a well-preserved building located in the Serra de Collserola, next to the hermitage of Sant Adjutori, through which the Roman road from Bàrcino to Sant Cugat passed. It dates from the Roman Low Republican period (2nd and 1st centuries BC), and was completely renovated in 2003. It is of Roman type and retains the square fire chamber and remains of the cooking chamber. The oven was intended for baking pieces of work and roof tiles.

Just in front of the Ermita de Sant Medir, on the other side of the esplanade, is the Font del Camp del Miracle. Made by APAC and the Penya Regalèssia, the fountain is presided over by a mosaic in homage to Sant Medir and the gathering that is held here every year at the beginning of March. Built of stoneware, it has four taps with a push button and a large running sink.

The hermitage of San Medín is a sanctuary located in the Collserola mountain range, in the municipality of San Cugat del Vallés (Catalonia). Every March 3, the traditional meeting of San Medin is held there.[1] It is near the old Roman road from Egara (Tarrasa) to Barcino (Barcelona), which passing through Castrum Octavianum (Sant Cugat) entered the Collserola mountain range. through the Gausac valley (or Sant Medir valley).
Formerly called San Emeterio, the hermitage is of Romanesque origin, although due to the transformations it has undergone there are hardly any original traces left. It has a rectangular floor plan with a sacristy. Outside there is a bell tower and the entrance door.
Peak of 430 meters included in the challenge of the 100 peaks of the FEEC. It is a magnificent viewpoint of Barcelona and the Vallès.
